key_insight: THz time-domain spectroscopy can non-destructively fingerprint and quantify specific grain-boundary defects in organic halide perovskite solar films using material-specific terahertz absorptions.
engineering_relevance: This enables closed-loop defect diagnosis and passivation for higher-efficiency, more reliable perovskite solar cells.
method: Measure 0.3–3.0 THz spectra of SVE-fabricated perovskite films
Identify intrinsic phonon modes and GB-localized defect vibrations from a THz library
Use oscillator strength to quantify defect concentration and guide passivation
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the 1.58 THz absorption in MAPbI3 scales linearly with XPS-quantified CH3NH2 defect concentration across four perovskite compositions.
watch_item: The fingerprinting approach depends on high-quality reference libraries and composition-specific calibration, so transferability to new materials or processing conditions may require revalidation.
paper_title: Terahertz Time-Domain Spectroscopy as a Universal Defect Fingerprinting Tool for Organic Halide Perovskite Solar Cells
